title: The Withered Fig Tree
subtitle: Christ comes hungering for fruit, and finds only leaves

stillness: Be still now, and draw apart.
As Ludolph counsels: withdraw from the road, and plant yourself in a hidden place.
Let nothing of the world be shared between you and it, this little while.
gospel: In the morning, at first light, returning to the city, Jesus was hungry.
And seeing a single fig tree by the wayside, He came to it,
and found nothing on it but leaves only.
And He said to it: May no fruit ever come from you again, forever.
And at once the fig tree withered away.
Now there were certain Gentiles among those who had gone up to worship at the feast.
They came to Philip and asked him, saying:
Lord, we wish to see Jesus, the Saviour of all.
And Jesus answered them: The hour has come that the Son of Man should be glorified.
behold: Mark the way of the diligent labourer:
in the morning He returns to the city, to preach, and to win some souls for God His Father.
He was hungry — not as a man needing bodily food,
for the evening before He had eaten, nor was it yet the hour to eat;
but hungry as the merciful God hungers for the salvation of mankind.
He still hungers in the morning, who hungers after justice.
He came to the fig tree not to find fruit to feed His hunger —
He knew that in March it was not the season for figs —
but for the sake of a miracle, to confirm the Apostles in faith.
See: this tree stood by the wayside, beside the road.
It had the leaves of words, but no fruit of works.
consider: The fig tree is the synagogue — many grains beneath one bark, men under one Law.
It stood beside the way, for it had the Law, yet it was not in the Way,
because it did not believe in Christ, who is the Way.
To it He came, and found nothing, though He sought long, with examples, teachings, signs and miracles —
nothing but leaves: the bare words of the Law, the traditions of the Pharisees, without any fruit.
By such a tree, green with leaves and barren of fruit, are the hypocrites and the false also understood:
standing outside the way of justice, wrapped in leaves by the pretence of virtue,
but strangers to the fruit of justice.
They have the outward show of holiness, yet inwardly are empty of the works of truth —
fruitless, and therefore cursed by the Lord, dried up, stripped of the dew of devotion.
As Chrysostom says: leaves of truth are only the appearance of holiness in a man,
religious words without any fruit.
Whoever lives beside this world can never keep the fruit of justice within himself,
for the demons passing and re-passing along the road shake it down.
If you would hold the fruit of justice to the end —
withdraw from the road, and be planted in a hidden place.
Every tree is known not by its blossoms, not by its leaves, but by its fruit;
and every man is proved not by the esteem of his name, nor by pomp of speech,
but by the witness of his own deeds.
This curse of the fig tree is to be feared above all in us,
because we do not turn our leaves — our words — into works.
For the axe is already laid to the root.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, grant me to have not only leaves — that is, words and the esteem of justice —
but the fruit of works, that I may never incur Your curse.
Grant that, as Your true servant, I may follow You according to my small measure,
that where You are, there I too may deserve to be.
Draw me after You, good Jesus;
let not the sweetness of the world draw me, but let the sweetness of Your love draw me.
Let my intention be ever with You in heaven, and let Your protection be ever with me on earth.
Let me cling to You inseparably, serve You perseveringly,
seek You faithfully, find You happily, possess You eternally,
my King and my God. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Si vis ergo fructus justitiæ ad finem tenere, recede de via, et plantare in loco secreto, ut nec mundus tecum aliquid habeat commune, nec tu cum mundo.
latinJewel.english: If, then, you would hold the fruit of justice to the end, withdraw from the road, and be planted in a hidden place, that the world may have nothing in common with you, nor you with the world.
